coredata.dc:title: CQDs-interspersed floral CdS/NiAl-LDH heterojunction towards efficient photoreduction of CO2-to-CO
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jia
Liu, Wentao
Li, Yang
Liu, Zhi
Liu, Yang
coredata.dc:description: A series of hierarchical flower-like CdS/carbon quantum dots/NiAl-LDH (CCL) photocatalyst have been successfully prepared to achieve efficient conversion of CO2-to-CO via a simple yet effective hydrothermal method. In the CCL, interspersed-CQDs not only increase the number of reactive sites but also reduce inner electronic impedance with the assistance of light reflecting-scattering effect. This significantly improves electron migration efficiency and inhibits the recombination of photoinduced electron-holes pairs. Without any sacrificial agent or photosensitizer, the sample of CCL-1 exhibits the highest CO evolution rate of 7.04 μmol·g−1·h−1 in water under simulated sunlight illumination with a large specific surface area (129.15 m2 g−1) and an appropriate CQDs content of 15.2 wt%, which is 12.8- and 11.2-fold higher than those of pristine LDH, pure CdS, and many previously reported LDH-based counterparts. A type-Ⅱ interfacial charge transfer mechanism is proposed, where electrons migrate rapidly from CdS to LDH with highly-interspersed CQDs as unhindered electron conduction bridges. The present work outlines a simple and controllable LDH-based heterojunction, which give rise to superior photocatalytic activity for CO2 photoreduction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NiAl-LDH
CdS
Carbon quantum dots
CO2 photoreduction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We sincerely appreciate the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21871124), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province, China (No. 2023A1515011007), and Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry (Jilin Normal University, Ministry of Education of China).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns, (b and c) FTIR spectra, and (d) Raman spectra of different samples.
TEM images of (a) pristine LDH, (b) pure CdS, (c) CdS/LDH, and HRTEM images of (d-f) CCL-1.
High resolution XPS spectra of pristine LDH, pure CdS, CdS/LDH, and CCL-1: (a) Cd 3d, (b) S 2p, (c) Ni 2p, and (d) Al 2p.
(a) UV–vis DRS profiles and (b) corresponding Tauc plot of different samples.

(a) Time course of photocatalytic CO2 reduction with water on pristine LDH, pure CdS, CdS/LDH, CdS/CQDs, LDH/CQDs, and CCLs, (b) CO, H2, and O2 yields of CCL-1 at various reaction temperature, (c) cycling tests of CCL-1 (d) isotope analysis of 13CO for CCL-1, (e) XRD patterns and (f) FT-IR spectra of CCL-1 before and after four cycles.
(a) Transient photocurrents, (b) EIS data, (c) Steady-state PL, and (d) TRPL spectra of different samples.
Mott-Schottky plots of pristine LDH and pure CdS.
Proposed mechanism of CO2 photoreduction into CO over CCL-1.

head.ce:title: CQDs-interspersed floral CdS/NiAl-LDH heterojunction towards efficient photoreduction of CO2-to-CO

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin Normal University, Siping 136000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Jilin Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin Normal University, Siping 136000, P R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shantou University, Shantou 515063, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shantou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shantou University, Shantou 515063, P R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, Jilin Normal University, Changchun 130103, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, Jilin Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, Jilin Normal University, Changchun 130103, P R China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of hierarchical flower-like CdS/carbon quantum dots/NiAl-LDH (CCL) photocatalyst have been successfully prepared to achieve efficient conversion of CO2-to-CO via a simple yet effective hydrothermal method. In the CCL, interspersed-CQDs not only increase the number of reactive sites but also reduce inner electronic impedance with the assistance of light reflecting-scattering effect. This significantly improves electron migration efficiency and inhibits the recombination of photoinduced electron-holes pairs. Without any sacrificial agent or photosensitizer, the sample of CCL-1 exhibits the highest CO evolution rate of 7.04 μmol·g−1·h−1 in water under simulated sunlight illumination with a large specific surface area (129.15 m2 g−1) and an appropriate CQDs content of 15.2 wt%, which is 12.8- and 11.2-fold higher than those of pristine LDH, pure CdS, and many previously reported LDH-based counterparts. A type-Ⅱ interfacial charge transfer mechanism is proposed, where electrons migrate rapidly from CdS to LDH with highly-interspersed CQDs as unhindered electron conduction bridges. The present work outlines a simple and controllable LDH-based heterojunction, which give rise to superior photocatalytic activity for CO2 photoreduction.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are well interspersed into CdS/NiAl-LDH (CdS/LDH) heterojunction via a simple yet effective hydrothermal method. Assisting by hierarchical 3D structure, light harvestability is significantly upgraded for the formed CdS/LDH heterojunction. A rational interfacial charge transfer with type-Ⅱ route permits rapid migration of photogenerated electrons through the interspersed CQDs, which endows CdS/LDH efficient photoreduction of CO2-to-CO.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic carbon dioxide (CO2) reduction is emerging as a promising avenue for environmentally sustainable development, focusing on converting CO2 into valuable C1 and/or C2 fuels, which has attracted substantial interest in recent years for a variety of compelling reasons. Firstly, it utilizes inexhaustible solar energy as a primary driving force. Secondly, it ensures minimal environmental impact and reduces overall energy consumption. Thirdly, it facilitates a mild and controllable reaction process, offering a distinct advantage over alternative methods such as thermocatalysis and electrocatalysis [1–3]. Given these benefits, there is an escalating demand to explore and develop high-performance catalysts for practical applications in CO2 photoreduction.

Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) have triggered considerable interests on CO2 photoreduction owing to their facile preparation methods, hierarchical three-dimensional (3D) structure with high porosity, and diversiform constitutive formulations [4–6]. Used as photocatalysts, LDHs offer several benefits for CO2 photoreduction: they exhibit remarkable structural stability, resisting framework deformation at temperatures below 200 °C; they possess an appropriate band gap conducive to CO2 reduction; their surfaces are abundant with hydroxyl groups for enhanced CO2 adsorption capacity; they facilitate the rapid release of gas products. However, several intrinsic tricky challenges, including low specific surface area, short fluorescence lifetimes, and limited capacity for harvesting visible light, still hinder their further release of photocatalytic activity [7–9]. Amongst the optimized strategies, introducing a second semiconductor into LDH to construct a LDH-based heterojunction has been widely testified to be feasible and still prevailing to address these drawbacks effectively [10,11], which is therefore labeled as the investigating principle in this work.

As an extensively investigated semiconductor, CdS possesses several advantageous properties for CO2 photoreduction, including a suitable energy band gap ranging from 2.2 to 2.4 eV, excellent electronic transferability, and strong harvestability of visible light [12–14]. For instance, Wei et al. explore a novel Co2+-doped CdS/NiFe-LDH heterojunction with enhanced photoelectrochemical activity and photostability for water splitting, attributed to the effective absorption and utilization of photons on CdS, along with the improved carrier density and separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers on NiFe-LDH [15]. However, CdS is prone to aggregation, resulting in particle adhesion, which in turn leads to an accelerated recombination rate of electron-hole pairs and drastically decreased available surface area, thereby deteriorating photocatalytic activity [16–18]. Therefore, it is imperative to further optimize the CdS/LDH heterojunction and fully unlock its potential advantages for significantly enhanced photocatalytic performance.

In the past years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), featured by their nanometer-scale dimensions, have increasingly been involved in various photocatalytic reactions. This integration aims to broaden the spectrum of light responsiveness, introduce additional catalytic sites, and enhance electron mobility [19–21]. Moreover, the surfaces of CQDs are rich in functional groups, such as -OH and -COOH groups, which can provide additional functions, including effective electrostatic binding sites for CO2 adsorption and conductive platforms for accelerated transfer of electrons. For example, using nickel phthalocyanine (NiPc) as a regulator, Chen et al. introduce NiPc-modified CQDs into Cu-In-Zn-S quantum dots to realize fast coextraction and transfer of electron/hole and exhibit significantly enhanced activity for benzyl-alcohol-oxidation-coupled H2 evolution [22]. Wei et al. utilize the capture effects of CQDs on electron and hole to optimize the electron transport path in CdS, which effectively addresses the issue of weak stability in CdS-based photocatalysts and demonstrates exceptional photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity and stability [23]. Considering these findings, it is reasonable to anticipate that the deliberate incorporation of CQDs into the CdS/LDH heterojunction would further optimize its structural and photoelectric properties, ultimately acquiring superior photocatalytic performance.

Evoked by the above recapitulative elaboration, our research has progressed to the design and characterization of CQDs-incorporated CdS/LDH (CCL) heterojunction for significantly enhanced photoreduction of CO2 into CO. The structural and electrochemical properties of CCLs with varied CQDs contents are investigated through a series of characterization techniques. Benefiting from the 3D floral morphology with higher light utilization efficiency from the reflecting-scattering effect, the optimal sample of CCL-1 exhibits the highest CO evolution rate of 7.04 μmol•g−1•h−1 within 4 h, surpassing those of LDH, CdS, and the related binary counterparts. The positive synergistic effects of CdS, LDH, and CQDs induce a type-II charge transfer pathway with effective electron migration from CdS to LDH.

Nickel nitrate hexahydrate (AR grade), aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (AR grade), ethylenediamine (AR grade), cadmium acetate dihydrate (AR grade), ammonium fluoride (AR grade), and ethanol (industrial grade, 99.5%) were purchased from ALADDIN Reagent Co., Ltd. Polyvinyl pyrrolidone (AR grade) and citric acid (AR grade) were acquired from Shanghai MACKLIN Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Urea (AR grade), oleic acid (AR grade), and thiourea (AR grade) were sourced from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company. All chemicals were used as received without further purification, and the aqueous solutions were prepared using deionized water.

0.53 g of cadmium acetate dihydrate was dissolved in 60 mL of ethylenediamine, to which 0.46 g of thiourea was added. After vigorous stirring for 30 min, the mixture was transferred to an oven and heated at 100°C overnight. The resultant yellow precipitate was rinsed with deionized water several times and then dried at 60°C to obtain pure CdS powder.

3.15 g of citric acid was dissolved in 72 mL of deionized water, along with 3.6 g of urea, and the solution was stirred for 30 min. This mixture was then heated in a Teflon autoclave (100 mL) at 160°C for 8 h, resulting in a navy blue liquid. Subsequently, this liquid was placed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight of 8000–14000) and immersed in copious deionized water at 60°C overnight for purification. Finally, the dialyzed solution was concentrated by evaporation to a volume of 50 mL to obtain liquid CQDs.

CdS/NiAl-LDH was prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method as below: 0.04 g of CdS power was ultrasonically dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water for 30 min to form the solution A. 0.22 g of nickel nitrate hexahydrate, 0.09 g of aluminum nitrate nonahydrate, 0.30 g of urea, and 0.07 of g NH4F were thoroughly mixed in 20 mL of deionized water to create the solution B. After mixing the two solutions with an hour stirring, the mixture was kept in a Tellor autoclave at 120°C overnight. The resulting precipitate was rinsed by deionized water and ethanol for several times, and hereafter denoted as CdS/LDH, possessing a Cd/Al mol ration of 1:1. In addition, two other CdS/LDH samples were prepared using the identical procedure but with varied amounts of CdS, 0.02 g and 0.08 g, resulting in Cd/Al mol rations of 0.5:1 and 2:1, respectively.

The targeted CdS/CQDs/NiAl-LDH was prepared following the same procedure as for CdS/LDH, with the additional step of introducing liquid CQDs into the mixture of solutions A and B, as illustrated in Scheme 1. The final products were denoted as CCL-t (t = 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2, respectively), where t represents the different dropwise milliliters of CQDs, corresponding to the CQD contents in CCLs of 7.6, 15.2, 21.2, and 26.4 wt%, respectively. For comparison, pristine LDH, binary LDH/CQDs (1 mL of CQDs on LDH) and CdS/CQDs (1 mL of CQDs on CdS) were also prepared as the identical process.

Sample characterizations in the aspects of structural and photoelectrochemical features, including powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectra, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra, scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS), N2-sorption, ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS), Steady-state photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra, time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), transient photocurrent, Mott-Schottky plots, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), are summarized and described in Support information.

Activity tests of samples on CO2 photoreduction were evaluated in a 200 mL quartz reactor without extra usage of any sacrificial agent and photosensitizer. On the top of reactor, there was a quartz window with 2.5 cm in diameter to receive successive simulated sunlight from a 300 W Xe-lamp. 20 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in 100 mL of water, into which CO2 was bubbled for 20 min to thoroughly displace the air. The reaction temperature was regulated at 15°C, 20°C, and 25°C, respectively, by an external condensation circulation system. Every hour for four hours, 5 mL of the internal gas was systematically extracted and analyzed using a GC-5890 gas chromatograph equipped with a hydrogen flame ionization detector and a thermal conductivity detector, provided by Nanjing Kejie Electrical Equipment Co., Ltd. Isotopic experiments were conducted to analyze the possible carbon sources in the products by utilizing the only raw gas of 13CO2 with the combination of mass spectrometry (MS-5975) and gas chromatography (GC-7920). The product selectivities of CO and H2 were calculated as the following equations:
(1)
Selectivity ( CO , % ) =
2
Y
CO
2
Y
CO
+ 2
Y
H
2
× 100
(2)
Selectivity ( H 2 , % ) =
2
Y
H
2
2
Y
CO
+ 2
Y
H
2
× 100
where
Y
CO
and
Y
H
2
are the yield of CO and H2, respectively. Preliminary testing results indicated that the CdS/LDH with an added amount of CdS, 0.04 g, exhibited the highest product yields. We therefore selected this sample to integrate with CQDs for the further investigation.

XRD is performed to investigate the detailed crystal features of the as-prepared samples, as shown in Fig. 1a. Pristine LDH possesses a series of representative diffraction peaks at 2θ = 11.62°, 23.38°, 35.02°, 39.4°, 46.96°, 61.24°, and 62.48°, which are indexed to (003), (006), (012), (015), (018), (110), and (113) lattice planes, respectively [24–27]. Seven typical diffraction peaks at 2θ = 25.06° (100), 26.7° (002), 36.56° (102), 43.46° (110), 47.86° (103) and 51.9° (112) are identified to pure CdS (JCPDS No. 75-1545). For CdS/LDH and CCLs, noticeable superimposition of XRD pattern of CdS and LDH is clearly observed, implying the successful integration of the two constituents without destroying their microstructures. Compared with pristine LDH and pure CdS, the XRD patterns in CdS/LDH and CCLs are obviously weaken and move to lower angles, which is stemmed possibly from a slight expansion of inter-gallery lattice spacing [28]. Furthermore, no discernable diffraction peaks of CQDs related to amorphous carbon are found in CCLs, most likely owing to high dispersion of CQDs on the surface of CdS/LDH. Fig. 1b records the FTIR profiles to analyze the functional groups of the as-prepared samples. For pristine LDH, three vibration patterns at 3470, 1365, and <800 cm−1 are attributed to the O-H bending-stretching vibration, asymmetrical stretching vibration of NO3 -, and translational vibrations of metal-oxygen (Ni-O/Al-O) and metal-oxygen-metal bridge bands (Ni-O-Al) within inner units, respectively [26, 29–32]. Due to the similar FTIR pattern of LDH in both CdS/LDH and CCLs, it can be easily confirmed that the LDH structure is well inherited in these samples. In contrast, pure CdS displays only two minor peaks at 618 and 1092 cm−1 due to weak signal response (Fig. 1c), which are attributed to the typical stretching vibration of Cd-S bond [12,13] but not detected in CCLs. Thus, Raman is utilized to further clarify if the constitutive CdS is integrated with LDH. As shown in Fig. 1d, two characteristic Raman peaks appear at 301 and 595 cm−1, corresponding to the first and second-order longitudinal optical modes of CdS, are observed in both CdS/LDH and CCL-1 [33,34]. All of the results suggest that the successful integration of CdS with LDH.

SEM is observed to illustrate the micromorphologies of the as-prepared samples. In the SEM image of pristine LDH, the observed floral microsphere can act as a hierarchical photocatalytic unit, which is consisted of regularly multi-degree arranged nanoflakes (Fig. S1a). CdS meets the benchmark of crooked nanoflakes like “potato chips” due to its cracked structure (Fig. S1b). Both CdS/LDH and CCL-1 exhibit a spherical appearance that the floral sphere LDH is covered, intercalated, and wrapped by CdS nanosheets, imlying that CdS is well-grown on the surface of LDH, and the introduction of liquid CQDs does not alter the microenvironment during this integration process (Fig. S1c and S1d). When incident light spreads on the surface of such hierarchical structured samples, part of the light is reflected off, and the rest is deeply scattered in the floral LDH, CdS/LDH, and CCL until being absorbed by them totally. This reflecting-scattering effect can significantly enhance light harvest capacity and increase the utilization efficiency of incident light [35,36]. Fig. S2 shows the HRTEM image, XRD pattern, FTIR profile, and UV–vis curve of CQDs, which present typical characteristics containing various functional groups (such as -COOH, -CO, -OH, etc.) with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm and a tinny size of less than 5 nm. To verify the successful addition of CQDs in CCL-1, TEM and HRTEM are conducted to observe the microstructures of the as-prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 2a–d, the TEM images of pristine LDH, pure CdS, CdS/LDH, and CCL-1 are coincident well with those of their SEM counterparts. In a partial magnified margin of the floral sphere CCL-1 (Fig. 2e), the incorporated CdS nanoflakes are observed, quite consistent with its SEM observations. In the further HRTEM investigation of CCL-1, three different lattice distances are measured to be 0.21 nm, 0.26 nm, and 0.316 nm, which correspond to the lattice spacings of CQDs (002), LDH (003), and CdS (100), respectively (Fig. 2f). These observations suggest the formation of intimate interfacial contact between CdS and LDH. The addition of CQDs into CdS/LDH, potentially acting as anchors on the surface, may enhance the interactive area and provide efficient electron conduction bridges between CdS and LDH, thereby boosting photocatalytic performance. The HADDF element-mapping images also unveil the highly-dispersed constitutive C, N, O, Al, S, Ni, and Cd elements rather than retaining aggregation status by simply normal mixing (Fig. S3).

N2-sorptions are performed to measure the textural properties of the as-prepared samples. As shown in Fig. S4, all samples exhibit typical type-II isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, notably lacking a distinct adsorption plateau at P/P0 approaching to 1.0. This discloses the existence of both mesopore and macropore segments. The corresponding pore size distributions in a widespread range to ∼100 nm also corroborate this point. By summarizing the textural properties of these samples (Table S1), it is found the integration of CdS with LDH almost has no contribution to the increase of specific surface area (SSA) for the formed CdS/LDH. After the addition of CQDs, the SSA of CCLs presents obvious increment with the largest SSA of 129.15 m2 g−1 for CCL-1. Overwhelming addition of CQDs would presumedly block and cover the porosity, thus resulting in the SSA decrease of CCL to 105.28 m2 g−1. This means that moderate addition of CQDs is beneficial to expand more porosity of the formed CdS/LDH to generate more active sites and aid light harvest, benefiting for the activity enhancement on CO2 photoreduction.

XPS analyses are employed to detect the surface chemical compositions and detailed valance states of the as-prepared samples. As shown in Fig. S5a, relative peaks of constitutive C, O, Al, S, Ni, and Cd elements in full survey spectrum verify the presence of these elements in CCL-1, in good agreement with the element-mapping results. The absence of N peak comes from the low amount addition of urea and NH4F during the preparation of LDH with a very weak signal response. Fig. 3 depicts the corresponding high resolution XPS spectra of Cd 3d, S 2p, Ni 2p, and Al 2p in the as-prepared samples. For pure CdS (Fig. 3a and b), two distinct peaks at 404.55 and 411.30 eV in Cd 3d are ascribed to Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2, respectively [37–39], while S 2 s spectrum can be categorized into two peaks at 160.75 and 161.85 eV, belonging to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 with the presence of S2- anion, respectively [40]. As to pristine LDH (Fig. 3c and d), the deconvoluted peaks at binding energies of 855.9 and 873.6 eV in Ni 2p spectrum and binding energies of 68.3 and 73.95 eV in Al 2p spectrum are correlated with Ni 2p3/2, Ni 2p1/2, Al 2p3/2, and Al 2p1/2 [41,42], respectively, which affirms that Ni and Al exist in the form of Ni2+ and Al3+ oxidation states. The O 1 s spectra, appearing unimodally at 531.25 eV, are related to the hydroxyl groups in all four samples [32] (Fig. S5b). Compared with pure CdS and pristine LDH, the deconvoluted peaks of Cd 3d and S 2p in both CdS/LDH and CCL-1 move to higher binding energies, contrarily, the fitted peaks Ni 2p and Al 2p in the two samples shift to lower values. Of note, CCL-1 presents the most significant shift for the four elements. These indicate that the electron density around Cd and S of CdS/LDH and CCL-1 are lower than those around pure CdS, while Ni and Al of pristine LDH has higher electron density than those of CdS/LDH and CCL-1. Such phenomenon results from the electron migration from CdS to LDH and the presence of CQD in CdS/LDH further strengthen electronic transferability, which is supposed to bring out superior photocatalytic performance. In addition, the peak intensities of Cd, S, Ni, and Al in CdS/LDH and CCL-1 gradually decline due to the decreased content of these elements after the introduction of varied precursors (ions ratio is changed), further insinuating the successful addition of CQDs into CdS/LDH.

UV–vis DRS is employed to evaluate the light harvest capacity of the as-prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 4, pristine LDH owns typical absorption edge located at 200–300 nm, 400–600 nm, and 600–800 nm, respectively. The former in the ultraviolet region is the charge migration from O 2p orbit to Ni 3d t2 g orbit (ligand-to-metal), and the latter in medium-long wave region correspond to d-d transitions of Ni (II) species in octahedral coordination geometry within the LDH frameworks. Specifically, the spin-allowed transitions of 3A2 g (F) → 3 T1 g (P) and 3A2 g (F) → 3 T1 g (F) within the 320–850 nm are caused by d8 configuration of Ni2+ ions in octahedral coordination. A valley appearing at 450–600 nm signifies the spin-forbidden transitions of 3A2 g (F) → 1 T2 g (D) and 3A2 g (F) → 1Eg (D) [43]. Pure CdS displays a strong absorption edge in the visible region around 475 nm, indicating responsiveness to ultraviolet light. In comparison with pristine LDH, pure CdS, and CdS/LDH, CCL-1 exhibits the largest adsorption peak and widest light-respondence region, meaning that its constitutive LDH, CdS, and CQDs give rise to positive synergistic effect to enhance light harvest capacity remarkably. Moreover, UV–vis DRS of CCLs has a slight red-shift when integrating CQDs into CdS/LDH photocatalyst, implying that the addition of CQDs into CdS/LDH provide more active sites for capturing UV–visible light and maximizing utilization efficiency of light irradiation. Derived from the UV–vis DRS spectra, Tauc plots are used to determine the band gap based on the equation of (αhν)n = A (hν-Eg), where α is absorption coefficient, h is the Planck constant, ν is light frequency, A is a proportional constant, Eg is the band gap energy, and n = 0.5 for a direct band gap semiconductor material. The Eg of pristine LDH, pure CdS, CdS/LDH, and CCL-1 are calculated to be 2.12, 2.3, 2.06 and 1.58 eV, respectively. The optimized band energy removes energy obstruction of photogenerated carriers transfer, assisting electron migration efficiency on heterojunction surface significantly.

Photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance of the as-prepared samples was evaluated in water without using any sacrificial agent or photosensitizer. The main reduction product is CO under same evaluating condition. As shown in Fig. 5a, CCL-1 with suitable band gap and excellent light harvest capacity exhibits the most CO yield of 28.2 μmol·g−1 within 4 h, which is 12.8-, 11.2-, 5.4-, 5.1-, and 4.0-fold higher than those of pristine LDH, pure CdS, CdS/LDH, LDH/CQDs, and CdS/CQDs, respectively, and even surpassing the previously reported LDH-based counterparts (Table S2). With the increasing content of added CQDs into CdS/LDH, the CO yields of CCLs present a trend of first increase then decrease, indicating excessive addition of CQDs would lead to overwhelming coverage of CdS/LDH interfaces to deteriorate active sites with insufficient light absorption and utilization. Water splitting could be a competitive reaction with CO2 reduction to form H2 and O2. In order to verify if CO2 reduction takes precedence over water reduction, the evolution rates of H2 and O2 over CCL-1 are detected and compared with that of CO. Meanwhile, two additional activity evaluations are tested at different reaction temperatures of 15 and 35 °C to explore potential thermal contribution to CO2 reduction. As shown in Fig. 5b, CCL-1 exhibits an evolution rate of 3.96 μmol·g−1·h−1 for O2 at 20 °C. The calculated stoichiometric ratio of CO evolution rate to O2 evolution rate is therefore to be ∼1.77, adjacent to the theoretical value of 2. The corresponding evolution rate for H2 is as low as to 1.08 μmol·g−1·h−1. Clearly, CO2 reduction is the predominant reaction, and the photogenerated electrons migrating from the valence band (VB) of CCL-1 are mainly consumed at conduction band (CB) to form CO but not H2. As the increasing reaction temperature from 15 to 25 °C, the evolution rate of CO over CCL-1 increases from 6.58 to 7.97 μmol·g−1·h−1, well confirming the thermal synergistic role to CO2 reduction. After four cycles, the CO yield over CCL-1 still maintains a high value of 25.0 μmol·g−1, and the cycled sample retains almost the same structure characteristics as the fresh one, indicating the good cycling photostability of CCL-1 (Fig. 5c–e). Several given blank evaluations under different conditions, including in absence of light, CO2, and H2O, respectively, are tested over CCL-1 (Table. S4), and no CO evolution is detected, suggesting the indispensability of these conditions. With 13CO2 instead of 12CO2 as the feedstock, 13C isotopic tracing experiment is performed to check that if CO2 is responsible for the origin of CO. As shown in Fig. 5f, five signal at m/z = 13, 16, 29, 32, and 45 are assigned to 13C fragments, 16O fragments, 13CO (reaction product), O2 (holes oxidation of H2O), and 13CO2 (excess input gas), respectively, well explicating that 13CO2 is indeed the source of originated 13CO and excluding the potential interference from the interspersed CQDs, degradation of interlayer CO3 2- anions formed in LDH or other adsorbed organics as a carbon source for the total yield of CO. In addition, CCL-1 is ion-exchanged using a NaH2PO4 aqueous solution (0.5 M) to the interlayer CO3 2- anions. Under identical evaluating condition as the untreated CCL-1, the treated-CCL-1 exhibits a CO yield of 26.7 μmol·g−1 (95% of its total yield), reflecting that photocatalytic CO2 reduction dominates the yield of CO.

Transient photocurrents, EIS, Steady-state PL, and TRPL spectra of the as-prepared samples are measured to elucidate the excitation-migration efficiency of photogenerated carriers, as shown in Fig. 6. During the preseted intermittent on-off irradiation cycles, CCL-1 possesses a strong photocurrent signal, CCL-1 exhibits a significantly stronger photocurrent response compared to pristine LDH, pure CdS, CdS/LDH, and CdS/CQDs (Fig. 6a). The smallest fitted EIS diameter also reinforces its lowest charge transfer resistance (Fig. 6b). Overwhelming CQDs would also presumedly leads to their accumulation or agglomeration, which is disadvantageous to its dispersion in CdS/LDH and weakening the capability of electron transfer. As a result, the resistance values increase with higher CQDs contents (CCL-1.5 and CCL-2), highlighting the superior excitation-transfer ability of photogenerated charge carriers on CCL-1 with more durable vitality of photogenerated electrons than those of others. Amongst all the samples, pure CdS presents the most intense PL emission with a prominent signal peak at ∼520 nm (Fig. 6c), reflecting the rapid recombination and low separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes [44,45]. In contrast, the PL plot of CCL-1 is nearly flat, indicative splendid charge migration efficiency between the interfaces of LDH and CdS. This can be further substantiated by TRPL spectra, where all curves are well-fitted with bi-exponential decays (Fig. 6d). The fitted parameters from the TRPL spectra are listed in Table S3. The mean charge carrier lifetime (τ) on CCL-1 is 6.55 ns, which is much longer than those of on pristine LDH (2.18 ns), pure CdS (1.7 ns), and CdS/LDH (5.7 ns), revealing that optimized CCL-1 possesses the prolonged lifetime of photogenerated carriers with dwindled behavior of electron-hole pairs recombination, which can potentially contribute superior photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance.

Mott-Schottky plots are applied to obtain the CB potentials of the constitutive LDH and CdS in CCL-1, as shown in Fig. 7. Between increasing frequencies from 1500 to 2500 Hz, the slopes of fitted plots display a positive correlation for both samples, feature of n-type semiconductor with the CB potentials approaching to the flat band potential. Derived from the intercept on X-axis, the flat band potential of LDH and CdS are determined to be –0.78 V and –1.1 V, respectively. Based on the empirical conversion formula from Ag/AgCl electrode potential to normal hydrogen electrode potential (NHE): E (NHE) = E (Ag/AgCl) – E0 + 0.059 pH, where E0 is 0.197 V at pH = 7. The CB potentials (ECB) of LDH and CdS are converted to be –0.56 and –0.88 V vs. NHE, respectively. In combination with the measured Eg values of pristine LDH (2.12 eV) and pure CdS (2.3 eV) from UV–vis DRS, the VB potentials (EVB) of them are therefore calculated to be –0.56 and –0.88 vs. NHE by using the formula of EVB = ECB + Eg [32]. Because the ECB of pristine LDH and pure CdS are lower than the theoretical CO2/CO reduction potential of –0.53 eV, and EVB of them outweigh the theoretical O2/H2O reduction potential (+0.83 eV), it is feasible for both of them to reduce CO2 in theory. After constructing CdS/LDH heterojunction, the photogenertated electrons accumulated on the CB of CdS flow to that of LDH, while the corresponding holes migrate from the VB of CdS to that of LDH, which well meets the type-II heterojunction mechanism. In the process of interfacial charge transfer (IFCT), highly-interspersed CQDs anchored onto CdS/LDH surface can act as numerous unhindered “electronic channels” to facilitate electron transfer and suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs. XPS results also shows significant peak shifts for Cd/S to the high binding energies and for Ni/Al to the low ones, indicating that the changed electron cloud density on them induces the migration of photogenerated carriers. Moreover, the hierarchical floral structure of CCL-1 with multi-angled nanoflakes endows it higher light utilization efficiency by the reflecting-scattering effect (Fig. S6), which not only permits that incident light multi-level reflection inside the catalyst to improve light harvestability, but it also provides effective supporting framework to expose more usable surface area and provide more surface-active sites [46,47]. Based on these, a plausible electronic migration pathway and mechanism are proposed as illustrated in Fig. 8:
(3)
CCL + h ν
→
CQDs
LDH ( h VB + ) + CdS ( e CB − )
(4)
CdS ( e CB − )
→
CQDs
LDH ( e CB − )
(5) 2H2O + h+ → 4H+ + O2 + 4e- (E0 = + 0.83 eV)
(6) CO2 + 2H+ + 2e- → CO + H2O (E0 = - 0.53 eV)

In summary, CdS and CQDs are well distributed on floral NiAl-LDH to induce a synergistic effect for enhanced CO2 photoreduction activity. Highly-interspersed CQDs with a size less than ∼5 nm act as unimpeded “electron migration bridges”, provide more active sites, and decrease resistance to facilitate the IFCT procedure. Supported by the hierarchical 3D structure, light harvestability is significantly upgraded for the formed CdS/LDH heterojunction. Without any sacrificial reagent or photosensitizer, the optimal sample of CCL-1 exhibits the highest CO evolution rate superior to pristine LDH, pure CdS, and the related binary counterparts for photocatalytic CO2 reduction. The IFCT process, following a type-II mechanism with the reflecting-scattering effect, is deduced and proposed, where photogenerated electrons migrate from CdS to LDH. The present study provides a meritorious instance on the construction of effective LDH-based heterojunction with the assistance of CQDs for efficient CO2 photoreduction, which will offer significant advantages for a wide range of photocatalytic applications.

Jia Li: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhi Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yang Liu: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Yang Li: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Wentao Liu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:Zhi Liu reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We sincerely appreciate the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21871124), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province, China (No. 2023A1515011007), and Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry (Jilin Normal University, Ministry of Education of China).
